coredata.dc:title: Effects of graphene quantum dots on microstructure, optical and gas sensing properties of coral-like ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Ebrahimi, Mahdi
Soleimanian, Vishtasb
Ghasemi, Mohsen
Nekoeinia, Mohsen
Mokhtari, Ali
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the composed nanocrystalline ZnCo2O4 and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared using the facile hydrothermal method. Various microstructure characteristics were specified using X-ray diffraction analysis approaches, including Williamson-Hall and Williamson-Smallman methods. The analysis of scanning electron microscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller, and atomic force microscope were used to morphological characteristics and the pore size distributions of samples. Besides, the optical band gap was decreased by the addition of graphene quantum dots to the ZnCo2O4 nanostructure. The greater crystallite size (45.0 nm) was accompanied by the smaller band gap (3.60 eV). The gas sensing investigations exposed that the coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures gas sensor exhibited high response (6.97–100 ppm), selectivity, and low detection limit (0.43 ppm) towards triethylamine. Moreover, enhanced gas sensing properties of coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures were observed when compared with coral-like ZnCo2O4 nanostructures. These results can be attributed to their higher pore volume and diameter than nanocrystalline ZnCo2O4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructure
X-ray diffraction analysis
ZnCo2O4/GQDs
Gas sensing
Triethylamine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanopowders.
The WH plots of β* vs. d* for ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD samples.

Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images of ZnCo2O4 (a) and ZnCo2O4/GQD (b) for 200 nm scale, respectively. The (c and d) SEM images ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD for 100 nm scale, respectively, and (e1-e5) energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) elemental mapping of ZnCo2O4/GQD.
EDS spectrum of coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) Pore size distribution curves of the prepared ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD samples.
AFM 3D images of (a) ZnCo2O4, (b) ZnCo2O4/GQD thin films.
(a) DRS spectra for ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanocomposite and (b) the (F(R)hν)2 vs. hν plots of these samples.
Response of the sensors based on coral-like ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanostructures to 100 ppm TEA at different operating temperatures.
(a) Responses of coral-like ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanostructures sensor as a function of TEA concentration (b) the linear relationship between response and concentration within 5–200 ppm.
Dynamic response- recovery curves of coral-like ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanostructures sensor for 20 to 300 ppm TEA at 200 °C.
The responses of two sensors to 100 ppm various test gases of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD at the operating temperature of 200 ℃.
The crystallite size, microstrain, and dislocation density of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD samples, calculated by WH and WS methods.
Analyzed BET and BJH parameters for coral-like ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanopowders
Comparison of TEA gas sensing properties in different works

head.ce:title: Effects of graphene quantum dots on microstructure, optical and gas sensing properties of coral-like ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Shahrekord University, P.O. Box 115, Shahrekord, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Shahrekord University, P.O.Box 115, Shahrekord, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Institute, Shahrekord University, Shahrekord, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Institute, Shahrekord University, 8818634141 Shahrekord, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University (PNU), P.O. Box 19395-4694, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University (PNU), P.O. Box 19395-4694, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Shahrekord University, P.O. Box 115, Shahrekord, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the composed nanocrystalline ZnCo2O4 and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared using the facile hydrothermal method. Various microstructure characteristics were specified using X-ray diffraction analysis approaches, including Williamson-Hall and Williamson-Smallman methods. The analysis of scanning electron microscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller, and atomic force microscope were used to morphological characteristics and the pore size distributions of samples. Besides, the optical band gap was decreased by the addition of graphene quantum dots to the ZnCo2O4 nanostructure. The greater crystallite size (45.0 nm) was accompanied by the smaller band gap (3.60 eV). The gas sensing investigations exposed that the coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures gas sensor exhibited high response (6.97–100 ppm), selectivity, and low detection limit (0.43 ppm) towards triethylamine. Moreover, enhanced gas sensing properties of coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures were observed when compared with coral-like ZnCo2O4 nanostructures. These results can be attributed to their higher pore volume and diameter than nanocrystalline ZnCo2O4.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, metal oxide semiconductors (MOS), including spinel structures, have attracted the attention of many researchers due to their unique fcc crystal structure with high lattice symmetry properties. The metal oxide with spinel structure can be divided into two types normal and inverse. The common formula for normal and inverse spinel structures are Atetra (B2)acta O4 and B tetra (AB)octa O4, respectively. In both structures, A is a divalent metal ion, and the component B trivalent metal ion.

These researches have been done in various fields such as electrochemistry [1], electronics [2–4], gas sensors [5,6], gas storage or separation [7,8], and so on. In recent years, many researchers have investigated the spinel materials in gas and catalysis fields because their excellent physical and chemical properties and unique structure especially 3D porous structure have aroused people's interest in gas sensing.

Recent studies show that p-type MOS have better performance in some applications than n-type [9]. In this regard, ternary metal oxide, zinc cobaltite (ZnCo2O4), a p-type MOS with inverse spinel structure, has been investigated in a variety of studies including, solar cells [10], supercapacitors [11] and gas sensors [12]. The different applications of ZnCo2O4 are due to its dependence on crystallinity, surface morphology, particle size, crystal lattice defects, porosity, and so on [13,14].

Researchers have used other common methods to improve the functional properties of spinels and achieve more desirable results, including compositions with nanoscale carbon structures [15,16]. Carbon materials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, quantum dots, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted special attention due to their high conductivity, large specific surface area, and low electrical noise [17,18]. In recent years, the researchers have provided the chemical functionalization of graphene and its derivatives by density functional theory calculations to investigate the electronic and optical properties of them such as structure stability, total dipole moment and energy gap. These parameters can be modified through edge passivation by different functional groups and elements [19–21]. In addition, many experimental efforts have been made to improve the physical properties of graphene derivatives using the metal oxide semiconductor compositions for various applications such as gas sensors and photocatalysts [22,23].

Among the carbon structures mentioned, GQD, an emerging carbon structure, contains a set of quantum dots (QD) and graphene properties. GQDs are one or more layers of graphene in dimensions of several nanometers. The unique properties of GQD and its combination with other materials have a high potential for various applications including photovoltaics [24], sensors [8], fuel cells [25], lithium-ion batteries [26], etc.

In this work, hierarchical coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD nanoparticles were fabricated by the hydrothermal method. The properties of the structure, microstructure, and surface morphology of ZnCo2O4/GQD composite were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Furthermore, the optical band gap of the sample was analyzed using the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). Then the gas sensors of both materials were prepared by drop coating method on silver electrodes and the gas sensing test was performed. The results show that the ZnCo2O4/GQD sensor has a high response and selectivity towards triethylamine (TEA), with poor cross-responses to different gases such as ethanol, acetone, formaldehyde, and ammonia. Besides, the coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD nanostructure sensor exhibits a good detection limit to TEA. With studies on the properties of the ZnCo2O4/GQD combination, it can be introduced as a suitable alternative to improve the performance of gas sensors.

The X-ray diffraction patterns were collected by Panalytical Xpert Pro PMD diffractometer at room temperature using the monochromated CuKα radiation (
λ = 1.541

Å
) in the range of 20–90 2
θ
. The step size and step counting time of the diffractometer are set to be 0.02 2
θ
and 60 s per step, respectively. The surface morphology of films is studied by SEM (model Zeiss Sigma). The diffuse reflection spectra of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanopowders have been recorded by means of a miniature spectrometer (UVS-2500, Phystech, Iran). The BET (BELsorp mini II) was employed for the characterization of the specific surface areas. The surface morphology and roughness of the films were characterized using the atomic force microscope (AFM) (Model: Veeco-auto probe-cp_research). Finally, the gas sensing properties were studied using the Nanosat GSCS-400 gas sensor system.

The procedure for preparing GQDs started with the carbonization of citric acid [27]. Typically, 2 g of citric acid was taken in a 10 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C in a heating mantle. After a few minutes, the color of liquid citric acid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange, affirming the formation of GQDs. The obtained liquid was neutralized to pH 7 by adding it drop by drop into 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution to prepare GQDs solution.

All precursor materials were bought from Merck Company which are analytical grade and without further treatment. The coral-like ZnCo2O4 was prepared by a facile hydrothermal method [28]. Typically, 1 mmol Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O, 2 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 5 mmol CO(NH2)2, and 2 mmol NH4F in 40 mL D.I. water under stirring for 10 min to form a clear pink solution. In a typical procedure, 5 mL of as-prepared GQD solution was added to 35 mL of the clear pink solution of zinc and cobalt precursors and the mixture was stirred for 10 min. The as-prepared solution was then sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the light pink solid was washed with D.I. water and ethanol for several times and then dried at 70 °C for 12 h. Finally, the samples were annealed at 400 °C in the air for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 to obtain the coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD. The pure ZnCo2O4 synthesis is similar to ZnCo2O4/GQD but without the addition of GQD.

The X-ray diffraction patterns of the ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD samples are shown in Fig. 1 . Both diffraction patterns correspond to the standard pattern (JCPDS No: 23–1390) and the cubic lattice structure of the samples with the space group F
d
3 ‾
m
was approved. The reflection peaks in the ZnCo2O4/GQD profile are obviously narrower and greater than the ZnCo2O4 profile.

By using X-fit software the observed X-ray diffraction intensities were fitted to the pseudo-Voigt function, and the values of the full width at half maximum (FWHM) and Lorentz coefficient (η) were calculated. According to the Williamson-Hall (WH) approach, the relation between integral breadth β* and the diffraction vector d* in reciprocal space can be formulated as [29]:
(1)
β *
=
0.9
<
D V
>
+ 2
<
ε 2
>
1 2
d *
where ⟨DV⟩ is the volume-weighted average crystallite size, and ⟨ε2⟩ is the mean squared strain. The WH plots of β* versus d* are shown in Fig. 2 , and two straight lines are fitted to the data. As is seen, the quality of fitting is rather poor (the R-square of fitted lines are 0.80 and 0.91 for ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD respectively), which is shown anisotropic strain broadening in ZnCo2O4 sample. According to the investigations of Ungar et al. [30], this can be caused by the effect of dislocations as a source of anisotropic strain broadening. Therefore, the dislocation density was calculated using the Williamson-Smallman (WS) approach [31]:
(2)
ρ =
2
3
<
ε 2
>
<
D V
> b
where b is burgers vector length and ρ is dislocation density. The size and strain values, achieved by the WH method, were located in Eq. (2) and ρ was computed for the reflections of samples in Table 1 . According to Table 1, the crystallite size of ZnCo2O4/GQD is greater than ZnCo2O4, however, the dislocation density of ZnCo2O4/GQD is less than ZnCo2O4.

The typical morphologies of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD coral-like nanostructures are characterized by SEM in Fig. 3 . The SEM images of the ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD are shown in Fig. 3a and b, which reveal both nanocrystals have coral-like shapes with porous surfaces. The grain size of the ZnCo2O4/GQD nanocomposite seems to be smaller than the other one, as exhibited in Fig. 3c and d. The elemental mapping of coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD nanoparticles was carried out, as shown in Fig. 3e1-e6. The results reveal that C, Co, Zn, and O elements are distributed homogeneously throughout the whole grains. The C distribution implied a GQD substrate all over the nanostructures as ZnCo2O4 spinels located on it. In the following, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite is indicated in Fig. 4 . The spectrum revealed that coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures consisted of Co, Zn, and O elements. The C element signal in the spectrum confirmed the presence of GQD structure in the composite. No other elements were observed from the spectrum that confirmed the high purity of coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures.

As shown in Fig. 5 , we carried out the BET measurement of nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution analysis of coral-like ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanostructures. As indicated in Fig. 5a, both samples represent a typical type IV adsorption isotherms with an H3-type hysteresis loop. The calculated pore volume and average pore diameter for ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD were 0.26 cm3 g−1, 17.1 nm and 0.46 cm3 g−1, 32.1 nm, respectively in Table 2 . The ZnCo2O4/GQD has a greater pore volume and average pore diameter than ZnCo2O4. Also, the pore size distribution curve is shown in Fig. 5b. This is revealed that the dominant pore size in ZnCo2O4/GQD is around micro and mesopore, while the pore size of ZnCo2O4 is in the micropore range. These results confirmed the SEM consequences in Fig. 3c and b. In Fig. 5b, a wider pore size distribution range from 5 to 25 nm can be observed for the ZnCo2O4/GQD, which can be attributed to gas sensing analysis.

Fig. 6 a and b shows the 3D AFM images of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD samples. AFM images clearly revealed the changes in surface morphology of ZnCo2O4/GQD as compared to ZnCo2O4. There is also a difference in the roughness of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD thin films. The average roughness of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD is found to be 10.4 nm and 11.1 nm, respectively. The variation in roughness is due to the interaction of the ZnCo2O4 molecule with the graphene quantum dots. However, the pore size changes have been observed in the FESEM characterization of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD grains, as shown in Fig. 3c and d, confirm the surface morphology changes, which have been revealed in AFM results in Fig. 6a and b.

The optical properties of the samples were investigated using the DRS m analysis method. Fig. 7 a shows the reflection spectra of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD. In the ZnCo2O4/GQD nanocomposite, the amount of reflection is lower than ZnCo2O4.

As shown in Fig. 7b, the energy band gaps of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanoparticles have been achieved at 3.69 and 3.60 eV, respectively. In nanoscale structures, according to Kayanuma investigations, the change in band gap (ΔEg) is relative to spherical quantum dots R, as the following equation [32]:
(3)
Δ
E g
=
E B
(
π
a B
R
)
2
where, EB and aB are exciton energy and Bohr radius, respectively. Also, if R > aB then a good approximation is R = <DA>/2, which formulates the variation of the energy band gap as a function of <DA>−2. According to Eq. 3, greater band gap for ZnCo2O4 (3.69 eV) than ZnCo2O4/GQD (3.60 eV) is with agreement with smaller crystallite size for ZnCo2O4 (26.7 nm) than ZnCo2O4/GQD (45 nm).

In the following, the ZnCo2O4/GQD nanocomposite was used to produce the TEA gas sensor. At the first, the optimum temperature working has been found. As shown in Fig. 8 , the responses of the sensors, fabricated from ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanoparticles to 100 ppm TEA were tested at different working temperatures. The response was calculated by Rg/Ra, where Rg and Ra were the electrical resistances in gas and air, respectively. Both of them responded at 200 °C as the optimum working temperature.

The variation of response vs. concentration has been shown in Fig. 9 . According to Fig. 9a, at all concentrations, response values are higher for ZnCo2O4/GQD than for ZnCo2O4. In both sensors, the responses increased sharply with increasing TEA concentration from 5 to 200 ppm (Fig. 9b), but the change of trend became slow and tended to saturation from 200 to 600 ppm, which could be explained by the theory that the surface adsorption was gently saturated with the increasing concentration.

According to a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 [33], the theoretical detection limit of coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD nanocomposite and ZnCo2O4 for TEA is calculated to be 0.43 ppm and 1.04 ppm, respectively. The smaller value of detection limit for ZnCo2O4/GQD reveals its better potential in more accurate detection of TEA at ppm-level.

As shown in Fig. 10 , the dynamic response-recover curves of the sensors towards different concentrations (20–300 ppm) of TEA were tested at 200C. Apparently, the ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanostructured sensors exhibit reproducible and reversible sensing behaviors. While it should be noted that the responses of the ZnCo2O4/GQD sensor to different concentrations of TEA are obviously higher than those of the ZnCo2O4 sensor. It reveals the positive effect of GQD on the gas sensing properties of ZnCo2O4.

Fig. 11 shows the selectivity of ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD sensing materials. The results reveal that the coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD nanoparticles showed a higher response than the coral-like ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles toward five tested gases. Also, the response value of the ZnCo2O4/GQD sensor to TEA gas is higher compared to the response to other tested gases and is about twice the amount measured by the ZnCo2O4 sensor. As shown in Table 3 , the response based on coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD nanocomposite, at a lower optimum working temperature, is more promising than other reports related to TEA gas sensors.
Overall, the coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD can give a comparable response, and it can be demonstrated as a good TEA sensing nanostructure at relatively low working temperature.

In summary, we applied a simple and reliable method to prepare coral-like ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles and coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD nanoparticles. We investigated the effects of GQD on the crystallite size and dislocation density using the XRD analysis and applying WH and WS approaches. The results show that the crystallite size and microstrain of the GQD added to ZnCo2O4 are larger than that of ZnCo2O4, however doped sample is significantly smaller than the pure one. The morphology and composition of the ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD nanoparticles were confirmed with FESEM, BET and AFM. The FESEM images showed higher porosity for ZnCo2O4/GQD than ZnCo2O4, which agreed with the increase in the volume and diameter of pores shown in BET results, and with AFM images. The optical band gap decreased from 3.69 to 3.60 eV for ZnCo2O4 and ZnCo2O4/GQD, respectively, which agreed with increasing the crystallite size in XRD analysis. The coral-like ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures exhibited a higher response (6.97) and a lower detection limit (0.43 ppm) to TEA than coral-like ZnCo2O4 nanostructures. The sensor based on ZnCo2O4/GQD composite nanostructures also showed good selectivity to TEA, which makes it to be a promising candidate for TEA detector.

Mahdi Ebrahimi, Vishtasb Soleimanian, Mohsen Ghasemi, Mohsen Nekoeinia and Ali Mokhtari contributed to the study conception and design. Sample preparation, data collection, and analysis were performed by all authors. The first draft of the manuscript was written by Mahdi Ebrahimi and Vishtasb Soleimanian. All authors commented on the previous version of the manuscript and revised version. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
